attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a chest X-ray that shows a 10% pneumothorax on the right. The chest X-ray had been ordered by his General Practitioner earlier that day after he had presented with persistent right sided chest pain and some mild shortness of breath after a coughing fit. He was sent to the Emergency Department after the diagnosis was confirmed on chest X-ray. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. He is a non-smoker with no history of respiratory diseases.

On examination, he appears comfortable at rest and on mobilisation. His pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 130/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. There are no obvious respiratory findings on examination. He is monitored in the Emergency Department for four hours and remains clinically stable.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate plan of management?
options: Discharge and book outpatient review with chest X-ray in two weeks
Commence patient on 1L supplemental oxygen and admit to hospital
Insert intercostal chest drain and admit to hospital
attributes.explanation:
This young man has spontaneous primary pneumothorax given he is a non-smoker, healthy and has no underlying lung diseases. The gold standard of management in these patients is observation for four hours then discharge with repeat chest X-ray and outpatient follow-up in two weeks to check for resolution of the pneumothorax. The patient should be given safety netting advice to re-present to medical attention earlier if symptoms persist or worsen.

Admission to hospital for observation would be warranted if the patient had shown any signs of clinical instability including: systolic blood pressure <90 mmHg, tachycardia, tachypnoea, hypoxaemia or severe chest pain or shortness of breath limiting mobility.

Aspiration of the pneumothorax is typically considered for larger pneumothoraces (>50%) or in patients who are symptomatic with smaller pneumothoraces. This patient has a small pneumothorax and is clinically stable.

Supplemental oxygen therapy may accelerate reabsorption of pneumothorax but is not routinely recommended for small, stable pneumothoraces in otherwise healthy patients who maintain normal oxygen saturation.

Intercostal chest drain insertion is reserved for larger pneumothoraces, recurrent pneumothoraces, or patients who are clinically unstable. It would be inappropriate for this small, stable pneumothorax.
learningPoints: Small, asymptomatic pneumothoraces in otherwise well patients can be managed on an outpatient basis.
references: eTG complete [Internet]. Melbourne (Vic): Therapeutic Guidelines Ltd; 2020. Pneumothorax [updated 2020 Dec]. Available at: https://tgldcdp.tg.org.au/viewTopic?topicfile=pneumothorax&guidelineName=Respiratory&topicNavigation=navigateTopic#toc_d1e171

Agency for Clinical Innovation. Spontaneous pneumothorax treatment options. Emergency Care Institute. Available at: https://aci.health.nsw.gov.au/networks/eci/clinical/tools/respiratory/spontaneous-pneumothorax/spontaneous-pneumothorax-treatment-options
Walker S, Hallifax R, Ricciardi S, et al. Joint ERS/EACTS/ESTS clinical practice guidelines on adults  with spontaneous pneumothorax. Eur Respir J. 2024 May 28;63(5):2300797.
